ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Aloo Tikki Chaat; closest parent 'potato dish' (FOODON_03302757) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Potato products and dishes) is the closest defensible parent; no specific Aloo Tikki Chaat entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Aloo Tikki Chaat or sufficiently close parent food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Aloo Tikki Chaat is approximately 250 g, comprising two to three potato patties topped with chickpeas, yogurt, tamarind chutney, green chutney, sev, and spices.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Aloo Tikki / Potato-based chaat composite
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for shallow-fried potato patties (aloo tikki) and boiled chickpeas separately. Values for Aloo Tikki Chaat per 100 g are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for aloo tikki (~180 kcal/100 g), boiled chickpeas, yogurt, and chutney components in proportions typical of a standard chaat assembly (approximately 55% tikki, 15% chickpeas, 15% yogurt, 10% chutneys, 5% sev and spices). The composite estimate is consistent with published Indian street food nutrient analyses in peer-reviewed literature (Longvah et al., 2017, ICMR-NIN).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — potato-based dishes and legume-containing mixed dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Aloo Tikki Chaat exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 based on analogous entries: shallow-fried potato patties (GI ~65 per Sydney GI Database) moderated by the presence of chickpeas (GI ~28, Sydney GI Database), yogurt (GI ~14), and tamarind chutney (low GI). The composite GI of approximately 55 reflects the legume and dairy components substantially lowering the overall glycaemic response relative to plain potato, consistent with published mixed-dish GI methodology (Atkinson et al., 2021, Diabetes Care). GL per serving = (55 × 24.5 × 250) / (100 × 100) = 33.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~775 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Aloo Tikki Chaat to occasional consumption, as the tamarind chutney, chaat masala, and sev contribute significant sodium (~775 mg per standard 250 g serving), representing approximately one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~55) and high glycemic load per serving (~33.7 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a moderate GI of approximately 55 (moderated by chickpeas and yogurt), the large carbohydrate load per standard serving (~61 g carbohydrate per 250 g plate) produces a high glycemic load of ~34, so individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (100–150 g) and pair with additional protein or fibre to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from yogurt and shallow-frying, combined with moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Aloo Tikki Chaat contains moderate saturated fat (~1.4 g/100 g) from shallow-frying and yogurt, and elevated sodium from chutneys and chaat masala; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150 g or fewer and request reduced salt in preparation to keep saturated fat and sodium within cardioprotective targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Tikki Chaat is a popular North Indian street food and snack dish consisting of shallow-fried spiced potato patties (aloo tikki) served on a bed of boiled chickpeas (chana) and topped with whisked yogurt (dahi), sweet tamarind chutney, spicy green coriander-mint chutney, finely chopped onion and tomato, chaat masala, roasted cumin powder, red chilli powder, crispy sev (fried chickpea-flour noodles), and fresh coriander leaves. The dish originates from the street food culture of Uttar Pradesh and Delhi in North India and is widely consumed across the Indian subcontinent and in Indian diaspora communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The texture is a contrast of soft, crispy-edged potato patties against creamy yogurt, crunchy sev, and tender chickpeas, with a flavour profile that is simultaneously tangy, sweet, spicy, and savoury. Nutritionally, Aloo Tikki Chaat provides moderate energy (~162 kcal/100 g), meaningful dietary fibre from chickpeas (~2.6 g/100 g), and plant-based protein (~4.2 g/100 g), but is notable for its moderate-to-high sodium content from chutneys and spice blends (~310 mg/100 g). The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low-moderate), moderated by the legume and dairy components. Clinically, the dish warrants portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its high glycaemic load per standard serving, and sodium monitoring for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is vegetarian and halal-suitable but not vegan due to yogurt, and may contain gluten from sev.

ingredient_synonyms.chaat: Indian street food snack with chutneys
ingredient_synonyms.chaat masala: Indian spice blend for chaat dishes

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Aloo Tikki Chaat as a composite dish exists; nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data (aloo tikki, boiled chickpeas, yogurt) combined in standard proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for component foods. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are primary approved sources and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Food name corrected from input 'Aloo Tikki Chat' to canonical spelling 'Aloo Tikki Chaat' (the word 'chaat' refers to the Indian street food category). 2. Input cuisine_tag 'Butter Chicken' appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and has been disregarded; this dish has no relationship to butter chicken. 3. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) component data; an exact single-entry match for the assembled chaat dish is not available in any approved primary source. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 5. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in shallow-fried vegetable preparations per ICMR-NIN data. 7. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is 'low' per the validator threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low). 8. Serving size of 250 g is a standard restaurant plate estimate; actual restaurant portion at Dugout - Circular Road may vary. 9. The food_group has been reclassified from the input value 'Vegetables' to 'Snacks' as Aloo Tikki Chaat is a composite street food snack, not a vegetable dish.
